schema.name: adventure-knowledge-graph
schema.description: A stateful, directed knowledge graph for a cyber-incident crisis-management gamebook: Gold Command talks a ransomware attack on a district council through a 72-hour statutory clock. Same executable rule DSL as every adventure on this platform.

graph.title: Gold Command: The Mereford Incident
graph.subtitle: A cyber-incident training exercise in judgement under a 72-hour clock
design_notes: There is no combat here. skill and luck carry the platform's standard test types instead: skill is technical/procedural judgement under pressure, tested at containment and at attribution, where the honest answer is ambiguous either time; luck is what the incident hands you regardless of judgement, sharpest at the recovery gamble, where refusing the ransom only pays off if the backups turn out as clean as the diagram claimed.

stamina is the one variable nobody puts on the incident dashboard: it only moves if Gold Command chooses to keep grinding through the night instead of resting, and running it to zero is the only way this story reaches ending_dead.

exposure is the sole bespoke stat, held to the platform's usual one-extra-stat pattern: a ratchet that only rises, standing in for how much of the incident becomes visible and reconstructable from outside the room.

cover_up_attempted and late_containment are the two ways a Gold Command can lose the story even when the technical response is sound; team_trust and public_confidence were cut as stats that never earned their keep, leaving the flags to carry that weight instead.

law_enforcement_call and cyber_insurance_call model two real levers most ransomware training skips: reporting to Action Fraud/the NCA is a genuine, low-cost decision with a mechanical payoff (a stronger forensics test) rather than flavour text, and the insurer's refusal to cover a ransom payment is drawn from how UK cyber insurance policies actually treat sanctions-adjacent payment risk, not invented for drama.

political_pressure's rumour_delivery is the closest a solo gamebook gets to the multi-stakeholder friction of a live tabletop exercise: two speakers, both right from where they're standing, neither of whom the player gets to cross-examine.

regulator_call and its flag (regulator_notified) refer to the ICO specifically, not to a bundled 'ICO/NCSC' action: the ICO notification is the actual legal duty the 72-hour clock enforces under UK GDPR, while an NCSC report is valuable, voluntary technical-advisory engagement with no statutory deadline of its own. The read_aloud narrates both calls happening together, as councils typically do in practice, but is explicit that only one of them is checked against a clock — don't reintroduce the conflation.

hours_to_deadline and comms_capital are load-bearing, not decorative: every route that represents genuine delay (verifying scope, waiting for forensic certainty, holding silence, the burnout grind, executive sign-off) spends hours_to_deadline, and spending enough of it locks the clean-recovery gamble (m080) behind a foreshadowed resource_at_least gate — dawdle for certainty and the option to refuse the ransom on your own terms can be gone by the time you reach it. comms_capital works the same way on the political axis: forcing an early service reopening or looping comms in unprompted both spend it, and spending enough forecloses the integrity route through political_pressure (m070), forcing bow_to_pressure instead. Neither resource should be left unread by a route ever again — that was the platform's earlier team_trust/public_confidence mistake happening again with a resource instead of a stat.
graph.narrative_premise: 06:10 on a Monday. The SOC has flagged mass file-encryption across Mereford District Council's shared drives. A ransom note names a 72-hour window before stolen data is published. You are Gold Command. Every choice you make in the next three days will be reconstructed later — by the ICO, by an FOI request, or by a journalist — whether or not it feels like it matters now.
graph.core_mystery: How far in did they get, and can you prove it before the clock forces you to guess in public?
graph.dramatic_question: Is the honest version of events worth more than the managed one?
graph.hidden_truth: The intrusion came through a third-party print supplier's VPN credentials, reused from a breach two years ago that was never rotated.

opening_context.player_facing_introduction: Your phone lights up at 06:10 with the kind of message that ends a normal week: SOC has flagged mass file-encryption across the council's shared drives, and a ransom note is already sitting in three inboxes, naming a 72-hour deadline before stolen data goes public. You are Gold Command. Nobody has briefed the Leader yet. Nobody has told the press office. Right now it is just you, a laptop, and a clock that started running before you woke up.

condition_dsl.combination: All conditions on a route must pass unless the route uses an explicit any block.

test_dsl.notes: 2d6 against stat plus modifier. skill is tested at containment and at attribution; luck is tested at the backup-integrity gamble and, optionally, at the burnout risk. There are no combat encounters.

regions.name: Mereford District Council, Incident Room
regions.theme: a 72-hour clock, a laptop, and every decision reconstructed later

narrative.surface_read: SOC dashboards, a borrowed meeting room, a ransom note nobody has stopped rereading.
narrative.deeper_truth: The technical incident is survivable; what actually ends careers is what gets said, or not said, while it's still live.
narrative.dramatic_function: Keep every choice legible as both an operational decision and a future line in an ICO report.
sensory_palette: a phone that will not stop
the ransom note's flat, rehearsed prose

nodes.title: 06:10, The Call
nodes.summary: SOC reports mass encryption across shared drives; a ransom note demands payment within 72 hours or stolen data goes public.
nodes.read_aloud: Your phone will not stop lighting up. SOC has flagged mass file-encryption across the council's shared drives, spreading fast enough that nobody has a clean count yet. A ransom note is already sitting in three inboxes, its prose flat and rehearsed: pay within 72 hours or the stolen data goes public. Every minute of hesitation lets the encryption spread further; every minute you don't spend, you can't get back. Whatever you decide in the next sixty seconds, someone will ask you to justify it in writing before the week is out.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: 06:10 again, in memory if nowhere else: the phone, the flat ransom prose, the clock that started before you were awake.

narrative.present_tension: Every minute of hesitation lets the spread grow, but acting on incomplete information can cause its own damage.
narrative.hidden_truth: The intrusion came through a third-party print supplier's VPN credentials, reused from a breach two years ago that was never rotated.
sensory_details: a phone screen that won't go dark
the ransom note's flat, corporate prose
cold coffee, untouched
narrative.emotional_undertone: The particular loneliness of being the first person who has to decide anything.

nodes.title: Cut and Contained
nodes.summary: Isolation lands fast. Spread is limited, but council tax, benefits payments and the print supplier link all go dark with it.
nodes.read_aloud: The order goes out and the network drops behind it: council tax, benefits payments and the print supplier link all go dark in the same stroke as the encryption's spread. It is the right call and an expensive one, and you will not know for hours which of those two facts residents will remember first.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The network stays down, quiet in the particular way of a system that has been switched off rather than merely broken.

narrative.present_tension: Containment bought you time; it did not buy you certainty about what already left the building.
sensory_details: dashboards going grey one by one
a silence where the helpdesk queue used to be

nodes.title: The Blast Radius Grows
nodes.summary: While you verify, encryption reaches the finance and benefits payment systems. Exposure rises.
nodes.read_aloud: Verification takes the time it takes, and the encryption does not wait politely for you to finish it. By the time the scope is clear, it has reached the finance and benefits payment systems. The number of people whose money is now delayed just became a number you will have to say out loud, later, to someone who wants to know why.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The blast radius as confirmed: finance, benefits, a spreadsheet of consequences that keeps growing while you read it.

narrative.present_tension: Verification was the responsible instinct; it was also expensive, and the expense is not yours alone to pay.
sensory_details: a scope document that keeps needing new rows
the benefits team's phone, ringing and ringing
narrative.emotional_undertone: Being right about caution and wrong about the cost of it, in the same hour.

nodes.title: Gold, Silver, Bronze
nodes.summary: Formal command structure costs you twenty minutes but buys coordination you'll need on day two.
nodes.read_aloud: You spend twenty minutes nobody wants to spend standing up a formal Gold, Silver and Bronze structure before touching the network switch. It feels like theatre while the fires are still small. It will feel like the only sane decision you made once day two arrives and six different departments need to be told six different things at once, in the right order, by someone whose authority isn't in question.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The command structure holds, minutes spent long ago on a decision that is still paying for itself.

narrative.present_tension: Structure now is time you don't have; structure later is coordination you won't have.
sensory_details: a whiteboard filling with names and roles
the specific relief of someone finally being told what to do
narrative.emotional_undertone: Discipline that looks like delay until it doesn't.

nodes.title: Whose Investigation Is This?
nodes.summary: Before forensics goes deep, someone asks the question nobody has yet: has anyone told Action Fraud and the NCA this is happening?
nodes.read_aloud: Someone in the room finally asks the question that's been sitting there since 06:10: has anyone told Action Fraud and the NCA this is happening? Reporting now, before the logs are even fully read, hands the investigation to people who do this for a living and puts a formal evidence trail under everything forensics finds from this point on. It also means outsiders are in the room while you're still working out what actually happened. Waiting costs nothing on the surface — until the day someone asks why law enforcement was the last to know.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The question is still open: law enforcement, now, or once there's something more definite to hand them.

narrative.present_tension: Reporting early buys formal support and a clean evidentiary chain; reporting late buys you room to work without an audience, at the cost of that same chain.
sensory_details: a phone number for the NCA's reporting line, written on a Post-it, not yet dialled
a duty log with a gap where 'law enforcement notified' should be timestamped
narrative.emotional_undertone: The particular discomfort of a call that costs nothing to make and everything to have skipped.

nodes.title: What Did They Take?
nodes.summary: Forensics works the logs. The exfiltration claim in the ransom note may be real, may be bluff — the evidence is ambiguous under time pressure.
nodes.read_aloud: Forensics gets to work on the logs, and the ransom note's claim of stolen data sits there unresolved: it might be real, it might be bluff dressed up to look real, and the evidence available inside the deadline is stubbornly ambiguous either way. You can push for a read now, on partial evidence, or wait for something closer to certainty — and the clock is charging interest on that choice too.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The logs are still being read, line by line, against a claim that refuses to resolve cleanly.

narrative.present_tension: Certainty and speed are trading against each other, and the trade only gets worse the longer you hold both.
narrative.hidden_truth: The exfiltration is real: the attackers pulled a benefits-and-payroll export through the same compromised VPN link three days before the ransom note landed.
sensory_details: log timestamps scrolling past faster than anyone can read them
a forensic analyst's coffee, also cold
narrative.emotional_undertone: The specific vertigo of a fact that exists but hasn't been proven yet.

nodes.title: The Silence Gets Expensive
nodes.summary: Waiting for certainty eats the clock. A local reporter is already asking why council tax payments have failed for three days.
nodes.read_aloud: Waiting for certainty eats the clock in a way that doesn't show up on any dashboard. On the third day of failed council tax payments, a local reporter starts asking why, in the tone of someone who already suspects the answer and is only confirming it for the record.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The silence is still costing you, in the specific currency of a reporter's patience running out.

narrative.present_tension: Every hour of waiting for forensic certainty is an hour someone outside the building spends filling the silence with their own theory.
sensory_details: a voicemail from the local paper, unreturned
the benefits helpline's hold music, looping
narrative.emotional_undertone: Diligence curdling into something that looks, from outside, like evasion.

nodes.title: Drafting the Line to Take
nodes.summary: Comms and legal draft a public statement. The version that protects residents and the version that protects reputations are not quite the same document.
nodes.read_aloud: Comms and legal sit down to draft the line to take, and two documents start to emerge from the same set of facts: the version that tells residents what they need to know to protect themselves, and the version that reads best in six months' time. Nobody in the room says out loud that these are different documents. Everyone in the room knows it.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The draft statement sits on the table, still doing its quiet work of deciding whose interests it actually serves.

narrative.present_tension: The gap between the two drafts is small enough to miss and large enough to matter.
sensory_details: two marked-up drafts on the same table
a red pen moving faster over one than the other
narrative.emotional_undertone: A form of dishonesty that never has to admit it is one.

nodes.title: The Leader Wants This Managed
nodes.summary: The Leader and Chief Exec want services back and disclosure minimised. 'Managed' is doing a lot of work in that sentence.
nodes.read_aloud: The Leader and the Chief Exec want services back and disclosure minimised, and they say so in the careful, deniable language of people who will not be the ones quoted. 'Managed' is doing a great deal of work in that sentence. Everyone in the room agrees residents come first; nobody says out loud what that means this week, because it means something different to each of them.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The Leader's office again, the same careful language, the same word — managed — still doing all the work it was doing before.

rumour_delivery.speaker: The Leader's special adviser, standing just behind the Leader's chair
rumour_delivery.text: Nobody is asking you to lie. We're asking you to lead with what's confirmed and let the rest come out on its own timeline. That's not spin, that's sequencing.

rumour_delivery.speaker: The benefits service manager, on speakerphone from the emergency payments desk
rumour_delivery.text: I've got people who haven't been paid in three days asking me why the council won't say what's going on. I don't have a sequencing answer for them. I have a Thursday.

narrative.present_tension: Everyone in the room agrees residents come first; they disagree, without saying so, about what that means this week.
sensory_details: a closed door with a raised-voice murmur behind it
a statement draft with more redactions than sentences
narrative.emotional_undertone: Consensus that is really just silence, worn by several people at once.

nodes.title: Going Public
nodes.summary: The statement goes out. Residents, press and the regulator now know what you know. The ransom deadline is still running.
nodes.read_aloud: The statement goes out. Residents, press and the regulator now know roughly what you know, at roughly the same time you know it, which is either the whole point of doing this properly or the last mistake you make, depending on who you ask by the end of the week. The ransom deadline does not pause to let the news cycle catch up.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The statement is out there now, doing its slow work in inboxes and newsrooms, impossible to call back.

narrative.present_tension: Once it is public, every subsequent decision is made in front of an audience that was not in the room for the earlier ones.
sensory_details: a press office phone ringing before the statement has finished sending
a comment thread filling in real time
narrative.emotional_undertone: The relief of honesty, immediately followed by the weight of it.

nodes.title: The Policy's Fine Print
nodes.summary: The council's cyber insurer joins the call before the ransom decision, and their position is blunter than the policy document ever was.
nodes.read_aloud: The insurer's incident line is answered by someone who has clearly had this exact conversation before. Their position lands flat and unambiguous: they will not indemnify a ransom payment to a group this plausibly linked to a sanctioned jurisdiction, and paying anyway is very likely to void the policy outright — the backup restoration and incident-response costs, on the other hand, are exactly what the policy exists for. It is, they point out, not really a coverage decision. It is the decision. There's one more thing they need you to hear before you choose: their recovery panel won't sign off on a restore attempt this late if too much of the seventy-two hours is already gone. Whatever's happened to the clock so far is about to become part of this decision too.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The insurer's position hasn't moved: covered for recovery, not covered for the ransom, and the two are not equally risky in ways that matter to them.

narrative.present_tension: The policy was written to make this decision look simple. Making it under a live deadline does not feel simple.
narrative.hidden_truth: The insurer already suspects the attacker is sanctions-adjacent from the ransom note's payment instructions alone; they are not guessing when they say the payment is unlikely to be covered.
sensory_details: a policy document, several tabs deep, open on a second screen
the insurer's loss adjuster taking notes audibly faster than the conversation warrants
narrative.emotional_undertone: The strange comfort of a decision that someone else has already priced.

nodes.title: The 72-Hour Clock, Kept
nodes.summary: The ICO notification goes out inside the statutory window — the one call on this list that is actually a legal duty, not a courtesy.
nodes.read_aloud: The call to the ICO goes out inside the statutory window, on evidence that is good enough rather than perfect. This is the one notification on your list that isn't optional: UK GDPR gives you seventy-two hours from becoming aware of a breach to tell the regulator, whether or not you're certain yet what happened. Somebody separately rings the NCSC's reporting line too, for the technical support and threat intelligence — welcome, useful, but voluntary, and nobody will ever ask you to prove you made that second call. Only the first one is checked against a clock.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The ICO notification sits logged and timestamped, a fact nobody can later dispute. The NCSC call sits alongside it, helpful and unrequired.

narrative.present_tension: A timely, imperfect notification will always read better in hindsight than a late, complete one — and only the ICO clock actually enforces that lesson.
sensory_details: a call reference number, written down twice to be sure
the strange calm of a deadline actually met
narrative.emotional_undertone: A small, procedural act of courage that nobody in the room will call brave.

nodes.title: Overrun
nodes.summary: The delay costs everything containable. Encryption reaches backups that weren't properly segmented.
nodes.read_aloud: The delay costs everything that was still containable. Encryption reaches the backups — the ones that were supposed to be segmented, and weren't, quite, in the way the architecture diagram had always promised. There is no clean floor left to stand on.

read_aloud_variants.text: The restore job stalls on the same backups the architecture diagram swore were segmented from the rest of the network. They weren't, quite. You already told residents and the regulator that refusing to pay was the responsible choice. It still might have been — but there is no clean floor left to stand on, and the statement is already out there, unable to be unsaid.

nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The overrun sits where you left it: backups gone, and no version of events where that isn't the headline.

narrative.present_tension: The last safety net you were counting on was never as separate as the diagram said it was.
sensory_details: a backup job log ending mid-line
a silence where a recovery plan used to be
narrative.emotional_undertone: The specific despair of a plan failing exactly where you were told it couldn't.

nodes.title: It Gets Out Anyway
nodes.summary: A journalist files an FOI request that the softened statement can't survive. The story is no longer the breach — it's the silence.
nodes.read_aloud: A journalist files an FOI request the softened statement was never built to survive, and the story turns overnight. It is no longer the breach. It is the silence around the breach, and silence makes for a much better headline than an honest technical failure ever would.
nodes.read_aloud_revisit: The story has already turned, and there is no version of the softened statement that survives a second reading.

narrative.present_tension: A cover-up only has to fail once to become the entire story.
sensory_details: an FOI response with a due date already passed
a phone that has started ringing from numbers you don't recognise
narrative.emotional_undertone: The specific shame of being caught managing the truth rather than telling it.

nodes.title: ENDING: The Playbook Worked
nodes.summary: Regulator notified in time, ransom refused, backups held. Services return within the week. The ICO takes no further action; the incident becomes the case study you cite next time someone asks 'is this worth the budget.'
nodes.read_aloud: Regulator notified in time, ransom refused, backups held. Services return within the week, in the right order, without a second crisis hiding behind the first one. The ICO takes no further action. The incident becomes the case study you cite, calmly, the next time someone in a budget meeting asks whether any of this was worth paying for.

narrative.present_tension: Resolved, at the cost of every easy shortcut you didn't take.
sensory_details: a services dashboard turning green, department by department
a debrief slide with no red text on it
narrative.emotional_undertone: The quiet, almost anticlimactic satisfaction of a plan that simply worked.

nodes.title: ENDING: Bought, Not Solved
nodes.summary: The payment buys a decryption key and a quieter fortnight. It also buys an NCA enquiry into sanctioned-entity payment risk, a voided insurance policy, and an internal audit finding that follows you for years.
nodes.read_aloud: The payment buys a working decryption key and a quieter fortnight than the alternative would have given you. It also buys an NCA enquiry into sanctioned-entity payment risk, a cyber insurance policy the insurer voids exactly as promised, and an internal audit finding with your name near the top of it that will still be circulating in meetings years from now, cited by people who weren't there. The insurer's loss adjuster, it turns out, took very good notes.

narrative.present_tension: Deferred, at a rate of interest nobody quoted you up front — the insurer quoted it, and you heard it, and paid anyway.
sensory_details: a decryption key that actually works, disappointingly ordinary to look at
a claim rejection letter, impressively fast for an insurer
an audit finding, filed and unforgettable
narrative.emotional_undertone: Relief with a second bill still to arrive.

nodes.title: ENDING: Overrun
nodes.summary: Benefits payments fail for weeks. National coverage. The ICO opens a full investigation; the Leader resigns before it concludes.
nodes.read_aloud: Benefits payments fail for weeks, not days. National coverage follows, then a full ICO investigation, and the Leader resigns before it concludes — not because anyone forced the point, but because there is no longer a version of staying that doesn't look worse than going.

narrative.present_tension: Ended, in the worst way an incident can end: everyone still has questions, and nobody in the room can answer them any more.
sensory_details: a national news chyron with the council's name in it
an empty chair where the Leader used to sit
narrative.emotional_undertone: The particular exhaustion of an incident that outlasted everyone's authority to fix it.

nodes.title: ENDING: The Cover-Up Was Worse
nodes.summary: The breach was survivable. The softened statement was not. You are named, personally, in the resulting report.
nodes.read_aloud: The breach, on its own, was survivable — councils survive breaches every year. The softened statement was not. The resulting report names you, personally, not for the ransomware, but for the sentence you approved that everyone in the room knew wasn't quite true when you approved it.

narrative.present_tension: Ended, with your own signature the last piece of evidence anyone needed.
sensory_details: a report with your name in a sentence you can't unread
a phone that has stopped ringing, which is somehow worse
narrative.emotional_undertone: The specific loneliness of being right about the technology and wrong about the truth.

nodes.title: ENDING: The Clock Outlasted You
nodes.summary: Another sleepless night at the incident desk finally costs more than the investigation gained. Someone else has to finish reading the logs.
nodes.read_aloud: You told yourself one more pass through the logs, one more sleepless night, and the incident would finally hold still. It doesn't. Somewhere around the fourth night without real sleep, the room tips sideways and does not come back. Somebody else finishes reading the logs. The incident does not pause to wait for you to recover, and neither, it turns out, does anyone's memory of who was meant to be in charge.

narrative.present_tension: Ended, by the one variable nobody put on the incident dashboard: you.
sensory_details: a keyboard, still warm, under someone else's hands now
a shift handover written by a colleague, careful not to say too much
narrative.emotional_undertone: The cost of treating your own limits as the one risk not worth logging.

routes.label: Isolate the network immediately
routes.stakes: Cut now, on instinct, and you either contain it clean or misjudge the scope and watch it spread anyway.
routes.hook: Every minute of hesitation is spreading; every switch you throw blind might be the wrong one.

narrative.player_intent: Act immediately on incomplete information.
narrative.dramatic_role: The judgement test that opens the adventure.

routes.label: Verify scope before cutting services
routes.stakes: Caution costs blast radius and six hours off the clock; verification takes time the encryption doesn't.
routes.hook: Cutting services blind has its own cost. So does not cutting them.

narrative.player_intent: Confirm the picture before acting.
narrative.dramatic_role: The cautious road, guaranteed to cost exposure.

routes.label: Stand up formal Gold/Silver/Bronze first
routes.stakes: Twenty minutes of structure now, paid back in coordination later.
routes.hook: It looks like delay while the fires are still small.

narrative.player_intent: Build command structure before acting.
narrative.dramatic_role: The investment road.

routes.label: Move to confirm exfiltration
routes.stakes: The ransom note's data-theft claim needs an answer before you can decide anything else — starting with who else needs to be in on the answer.
routes.hook: The note says data left the building. The note also wants your money.

narrative.player_intent: Begin the investigation.
narrative.dramatic_role: Hub spoke to the investigation branch.

routes.label: Report to Action Fraud and the NCA now, before forensics goes any deeper
routes.stakes: An outside agency in the room this early, in exchange for a formal, defensible evidence chain from here on.
routes.hook: The reporting line takes four minutes to answer. It costs you nothing but the interruption.

narrative.player_intent: Engage law enforcement before the forensic work proceeds.
narrative.dramatic_role: The procedurally sound but socially costly choice; strengthens the forensics test that follows.

routes.label: Handle it internally for now; report later if it's confirmed
routes.stakes: Working without an audience, at the cost of a weaker evidence trail if this ever needs proving to someone else.
routes.hook: Nobody has actually decided not to report it. It just hasn't happened yet.

narrative.player_intent: Delay law enforcement engagement.
narrative.dramatic_role: The quieter road; weakens the forensics test and nudges exposure regardless of outcome.

routes.label: Push to reopen benefits payments before forensics finish
routes.stakes: Residents need their payments; forensics needs the network exactly as it is — and forcing the issue this early spends political capital you may want later.
routes.hook: The benefits helpline has stopped ringing. That is not the same as stopped needing.

narrative.player_intent: Prioritise service restoration over investigation.
narrative.dramatic_role: Early route into the political pressure encounter.

routes.label: Isolate now, later than you should have
routes.stakes: Contained, finally — but the record will show you waited.
routes.hook: The switch is still there. It always was.

narrative.player_intent: Contain the incident, belatedly.
narrative.dramatic_role: The delayed correction; marks late_containment permanently.

routes.label: Try to buy time before isolating
routes.stakes: Every extra minute of uncontained spread is a minute you can't get back, and this one costs half a day of runway.
routes.hook: The backups are supposed to be segmented from this. Supposed to be.

narrative.player_intent: Delay containment further.
narrative.dramatic_role: The road to the overrun ending.

routes.label: Isolate the network now
routes.stakes: Structure is in place; now the network comes down.
routes.hook: Gold, Silver and Bronze all agree: cut it.

narrative.player_intent: Contain with formal structure already standing.
narrative.dramatic_role: The disciplined containment route.

routes.label: Verify scope first
routes.stakes: Even with structure standing, verification still costs blast radius.
routes.hook: Command structure doesn't slow the encryption down. Only isolation does that.

narrative.player_intent: Verify before acting, with structure in place.
narrative.dramatic_role: The cautious route out of the structured branch.

routes.label: Work the logs for a definitive answer
routes.stakes: A clean read of the logs, worked alone, turns a suspicion into a fact you can put in a regulator notification — if you can read it right without help, in the four hours it takes.
routes.hook: The timestamps are all there. Reading them right, under this clock, is the hard part.

narrative.player_intent: Establish whether data actually left the network, without outside support.
narrative.dramatic_role: The core investigative test, run at the harder difficulty available when law enforcement wasn't looped in.

routes.label: Work the logs for a definitive answer, with the NCA's technical support
routes.stakes: A clean read of the logs turns a suspicion into a fact you can put in a regulator notification — and this time you're not reading them alone.
routes.hook: The NCA's analyst has seen this exact log pattern before. That's worth more than another pot of coffee.

narrative.player_intent: Establish whether data actually left the network, with formal backup.
narrative.dramatic_role: The reward for the law-enforcement decision: a meaningfully better test at the adventure's core investigation.

routes.label: Notify the ICO now, on partial evidence
routes.stakes: Notify inside the statutory window on the evidence you have, imperfect as it is — this is the one call the clock actually measures.
routes.hook: A timely, imperfect notification reads better in every report that follows.

narrative.player_intent: Notify the ICO immediately, inside the legal deadline.
narrative.dramatic_role: The fast, defensible compliance route.

routes.label: Wait for forensic certainty before notifying
routes.stakes: Certainty is worth having. It is not free — a full day of the clock, and the option to still refuse the ransom on your own terms may not survive the wait.
routes.hook: Somewhere outside this room, a reporter is already noticing the silence.

narrative.player_intent: Delay notification for a stronger evidence base.
narrative.dramatic_role: The road that lets exposure and the press cycle catch up.

routes.label: Loop in comms before deciding
routes.stakes: Bringing comms in early shapes what gets said before the decision is even made, and spends some of the goodwill you'll want when the Leader pushes back.
routes.hook: The line to take is being drafted whether or not you've decided what the line is.

narrative.player_intent: Involve comms before committing to a disclosure path.
narrative.dramatic_role: Route into the statement-drafting branch.

routes.label: Keep working through the night instead of resting
routes.stakes: Another pass through the logs without sleep might catch what you missed, at the cost of a third of a day. It might also be the pass that breaks you.
routes.hook: Nobody is going to tell you to stop. That's exactly the problem.

narrative.player_intent: Push past exhaustion for another shot at the evidence.
narrative.dramatic_role: The optional, repeatable risk that can end the run outright.

routes.label: Notify the ICO now, later than the statutory window
routes.stakes: Late is worse than on time, and considerably better than never.
routes.hook: The seventy-two hours closed while you were waiting for certainty. Notify anyway.

narrative.player_intent: Notify the ICO despite the missed statutory deadline.
narrative.dramatic_role: The recovery route out of delay.

routes.label: Hold the line on silence a while longer
routes.stakes: Silence held past the point of plausibility stops looking like caution, and costs another day you don't have.
routes.hook: The reporter's questions aren't going away because you don't answer them.

narrative.player_intent: Continue withholding disclosure.
narrative.dramatic_role: The road into the cover-up ending.

routes.label: Publish the transparent version
routes.stakes: The version that protects residents goes out, as written.
routes.hook: Legal signed off. It just didn't sign off enthusiastically.

narrative.player_intent: Publish the honest statement.
narrative.dramatic_role: The transparency route.

routes.label: Hold for exec sign-off first
routes.stakes: Sign-off is procedure. It is also an opportunity for the statement to change hands, and six hours you don't get back.
routes.hook: The Leader's office has asked to see it before it goes anywhere.

narrative.player_intent: Route the statement through executive approval.
narrative.dramatic_role: Route into the political pressure encounter.

routes.label: Hold the line on full, timely disclosure
routes.foreshadow: Holding this line in front of the Leader takes political capital, not just conviction — and if you already spent yours winning an earlier argument, you won't have enough left to win this one.
routes.stakes: Refusing to soften the statement costs you politically, here, tonight — and is the only version of this evening nobody has to lie about later.
routes.hook: 'Managed' was never going to be a word you could put your name to.

narrative.player_intent: Resist pressure to soften the statement.
narrative.dramatic_role: The integrity route through the pivotal encounter — gated on comms_capital, so spending goodwill earlier can foreclose it.

routes.label: Soften the statement to protect the political leadership
routes.stakes: The softened version buys the Leader a quieter week and buys you a much worse one later.
routes.hook: It only has to fail once for the softening to become the whole story.

narrative.player_intent: Yield to political pressure.
narrative.dramatic_role: The road into the cover-up ending.

routes.label: Loop in the council's cyber insurer before deciding on the ransom
routes.stakes: The policy has an opinion on this decision whether or not you ask for it; better to hear it before you choose.
routes.hook: Someone in finance has already found the incident-response hotline number in the policy documents.

narrative.player_intent: Consult the insurer before the ransom decision.
narrative.dramatic_role: Hub spoke into the insurance-informed finale.

routes.label: Refuse to pay, restore from backup
routes.foreshadow: A full restore-and-verify cycle needs real runway — the recovery team is honest that they can't safely attempt it on whatever's left of the clock once you're this far into the seventy-two hours.
routes.stakes: Refusing the ransom bets everything on the backups actually being clean — the diagram said segmented, and no amount of judgement gets you a better answer than the restore job itself. At least the policy covers the attempt, and there's still enough clock left to risk it.
routes.hook: The backups were segmented on the diagram. Now you find out if they were segmented in fact.

narrative.player_intent: Refuse the ransom and recover independently.
narrative.dramatic_role: The clean-recovery finale gamble; the one moment fortune, not judgement, decides the outcome — and only available if earlier delays haven't already spent the clock it needs.

routes.label: Authorise a quiet ransom payment to buy certainty, against the insurer's advice
routes.stakes: A quiet payment buys speed and certainty, creates a paper trail of its own, and — the insurer was clear about this part — leaves the bill uncovered.
routes.hook: Quiet is doing a lot of work in 'quiet payment'.

narrative.player_intent: Pay the ransom against the insurer's explicit advice.
narrative.dramatic_role: The mixed-outcome finale road; now costed correctly against the policy that won't cover it.

routes.label: Move to public disclosure
routes.stakes: The regulator knows. Residents and press are next.
routes.hook: A notification logged with the ICO is not the same as a notification the public has seen.

narrative.player_intent: Proceed to public disclosure after notifying the regulator.
narrative.dramatic_role: Hub spoke to the public disclosure endgame.

routes.label: Assess the damage
routes.stakes: There is no version of this assessment that comes back small.
routes.hook: The backups are gone. There is nothing left to contain.

narrative.player_intent: Face the full scope of the overrun.
narrative.dramatic_role: Route into the catastrophic ending.

routes.label: Face the fallout
routes.stakes: The story has already turned. There is no statement left that changes that.
routes.hook: The FOI response is due, and there is no answer that doesn't confirm what the journalist already suspects.

narrative.player_intent: Confront the consequences of the exposed cover-up.
narrative.dramatic_role: Route into the scandal ending.
